title: Public Relations

jokes.text: The Milford School credo: children should be 'neither seen nor heard' — followed immediately by the headmaster saying 'Woman.'
jokes.setup: The narrator introduces the Milford School as prestigious with the high-minded credo that children be 'neither seen nor heard.'
jokes.punchline: The very next word out of the headmaster's mouth is 'Woman.' — a single dismissive word that proves the school's philosophy extends to adults too, or simply reveals the headmaster's character instantly.
jokes.explanation: The joke is a masterclass in economy — one word does all the work. The setup primes us for something dignified and then immediately undercuts it. The 'neither seen nor heard' credo is already inherently absurd, so the punchline lands as a perfect reveal of the school's true ethos. Rewatch bonus because the phrase becomes important later in the episode with Buster.

jokes.text: Michael tells George Michael the next four years are about his future, then the headmaster reveals they now have a 'talking room' where students can talk to their heart's content, and George Michael's first words all day are 'Sounds like fun.'
jokes.setup: The Milford School is famous for its 'neither seen nor heard' credo. The headmaster now says they've modernized with talking sessions and a talking room.
jokes.punchline: George Michael has been silent all day — and his very first words are approving 'Sounds like fun,' which the headmaster then notes is the first thing he's said all day.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple levels: it characterizes George Michael as naturally fitting the Milford mold (already neither seen nor heard), it's ironic that his only words praise the school's new talking feature, and the headmaster's deadpan observation seals it. Strong character integration — this specific joke only works with George Michael's shy disposition.

jokes.text: 'Save it for the talk room.' The headmaster shushes Buster's enthusiasm mid-sentence during the flashback.
jokes.setup: The headmaster is pitching the new progressive talking room to Michael and George Michael.
jokes.punchline: When Buster pipes up enthusiastically in the memory/flashback, the headmaster immediately shuts him down — 'Save it for the talk room' — perfectly contradicting the stated open-door policy.
jokes.explanation: The irony is crisp: the man selling a talking room immediately suppresses talking. It also establishes Buster's history with the school before the punchline reveal. Economy is excellent — four words do a lot of work.

jokes.text: Michael assures the headmaster his family is 'back on track and no longer in any trouble' — cut immediately to the newspaper headline about the Klimpy's brawl.
jokes.setup: Michael confidently tells the headmaster the family is no longer in trouble.
jokes.punchline: The headmaster immediately produces today's paper, and we cut to the Klimpy's incident, proving Michael completely wrong in real time.
jokes.explanation: A reliable comedic device — character makes a confident claim, immediately undercut by reality. Well-executed here with the visual of the newspaper and the immediate cut. Not highly original but structurally clean.

jokes.text: 'Don't ever mention Klimpy's to me again.' / 'If I still had money, I'd buy a Klimpy's just to burn it to the ground.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks what happened. Lucille begins with dramatic hatred of Klimpy's.
jokes.punchline: Her escalation from 'don't mention it' to 'I'd buy it just to burn it down' reveals the depth of her grievance before we even know what happened — and the 'if I still had money' clause reminds us of the family's fall from grace.
jokes.explanation: Pure Lucille — the disproportionate rage, the casual acknowledgment of their poverty as an afterthought, the melodrama. Character integration is very high; this line couldn't come from anyone else. The 'if I still had money' subclause does double duty as character comedy and irony.

jokes.text: At Klimpy's, waitress asks 'Plate or platter?' and Lucille replies: 'I don't understand the question, and I won't respond to it.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is forced to slum it at Klimpy's, a restaurant far beneath her station. The waitress asks a completely normal question.
jokes.punchline: Lucille treats a routine menu question as an incomprehensible affront, refusing to engage with it — as if 'plate or platter' is philosophically incoherent.
jokes.explanation: One of the great Lucille Bluth lines in the series. The joke is exquisitely character-specific: her contempt for the working class, her entitlement, her refusal to adapt. 'I don't understand the question' (she clearly does) followed by 'and I won't respond to it' (she is responding) is internally contradictory in the most regal possible way. Perfect economy, perfect structure, extremely quotable, and could only come from Lucille. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor in the prompt — this exceeds it due to the perfect character integration and quotability that made it a cultural touchstone.

jokes.text: Lindsay tells the waitress at Klimpy's: 'I'd like my old life back.'
jokes.setup: The waitress asks Lindsay what she'd like to order.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay answers as if the waitress has asked a larger existential question about what she wants, expressing her wish to escape poverty rather than ordering food.
jokes.explanation: A compact, elegant joke. Lindsay's self-pity is perfectly deployed in a single line that misdirects the mundane question into existential complaint. Strong character integration — Lindsay's inability to adapt to her diminished circumstances is her core trait. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: 'Everything I've said about you can be covered with makeup and a lie about a thyroid problem.'
jokes.setup: A fight between Lucille and Lindsay at Klimpy's is being described — Lucille claims she said only 'one tiny thing' at the end.
jokes.punchline: The 'tiny thing' turns out to be a withering critique of Lindsay's appearance, offered up with the casual suggestion that it can be cosmetically concealed.
jokes.explanation: Lucille at her cruellest and funniest. The line is multifaceted: it implies she's been saying horrible things about Lindsay's looks all night, that this is somehow acceptable if concealed, and that a 'thyroid problem' is a well-worn excuse for weight gain. The specificity ('thyroid problem') elevates it above generic cruelty. This rivals the 8.0 anchor — perfectly character-specific, genuinely dark, highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'Good grief almighty! You think I'm enjoying my slide into poverty?' — Lucille in the middle of a food fight at Klimpy's.
jokes.setup: A brawl has broken out at Klimpy's, apparently involving cake.
jokes.punchline: In the middle of a chaos she helped create, Lucille delivers a line that's both self-pitying and completely out of touch with the actual crisis at hand.
jokes.explanation: Solidly funny — the timing (mid-brawl) and the obliviousness are classic Lucille, but it's a slight step below her sharpest lines. The misdirection between her personal grievance and the physical chaos around her works well.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Good grief almighty! You think I'm enjoying my slide into poverty?'
jokes.setup: Lucille is in a fight at Klimpy's budget restaurant.
jokes.punchline: Lucille frames eating at Klimpy's as a 'slide into poverty,' treating a minor indignity as civilizational collapse — her grandiosity framing budget dining as Dickensian destitution.
jokes.explanation: Reliably funny Lucille moment — her class anxiety expressed as righteous victimhood. 'Slide into poverty' is a great phrase for her. Slightly less surprising than other Lucille lines in the episode.

jokes.text: 'Sorry, Lindsay. There goes your dessert.' — Narrator's deadpan as food is apparently thrown during the fight.
jokes.setup: A food fight breaks out at Klimpy's involving cake.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's dry observation reduces the chaos to a comment about Lindsay's dessert, implying she cares more about food than the brawl — and gently referencing Lucille's prior body-shaming.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan voice is one of the show's defining features. This is a solid deployment — simultaneously observational, cruel (by implication), and economical. The callback to the body image subtext adds a layer.

jokes.text: 'Oh, big deal. Buster's the only one who ever liked it.' — Lucille dismisses George Michael losing his chance at Milford.
jokes.setup: Michael says the Klimpy's brawl cost George Michael a chance at the prestigious Milford School.
jokes.punchline: Lucille is completely unbothered, and her response immediately sets up the Buster flashback.
jokes.explanation: Functions primarily as a setup for the Buster gag that follows, but works as a standalone Lucille dismissal of her grandchild's future. Rewatch bonus because it's also foreshadowing the Buster payoff.

jokes.text: Buster so excelled at being 'neither seen nor heard' that he remained at the Milford School undetected for a full two semesters after he was supposed to graduate.
jokes.setup: Lucille mentions Buster liked the Milford School. The school's credo is 'neither seen nor heard.'
jokes.punchline: Buster was so perfectly suited to the school's philosophy that he literally disappeared into it — undetected for two semesters post-graduation.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional writing. The Milford credo ('neither seen nor heard') is established as a joke in itself, and then the callback reveals that Buster — the most dependent, invisible, childlike adult in the family — literally embodies it to a surreal degree. The absurdist logic (staying undetected for two extra semesters) is grounded in perfect character truth. This is the kind of joke Arrested Development is celebrated for: it rewards the setup, deepens character, and is genuinely funny on its own. Justifiably scores near the top of this episode.

jokes.text: 'Michael, if this is a lecture on how we're all supposed to whatever and blah, blah, blah, well, you can save it 'cause we all know it by heart.'
jokes.setup: Michael is about to address the family about the bad press.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay preemptively dismisses his speech by reducing it to 'whatever and blah, blah, blah' — showing she's heard it so many times she can't even be bothered to parody it specifically.
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's lazy contempt for Michael's responsibility speeches is perfectly captured. The vagueness ('whatever and blah, blah, blah') is the joke — it shows she's tuned out so completely she can't even articulate what she's dismissing. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Lucille is eating a raw onion like an apple at the family meeting. When asked 'Is this your onion?' she simply says 'Yes.'
jokes.setup: The family gathers for a meeting at the model home.
jokes.punchline: Lucille is casually eating a whole raw onion as if it's completely normal. When asked about it, she offers no explanation whatsoever.
jokes.explanation: A pure visual/behavioral gag that is quintessentially absurdist. There is no explanation — Lucille eats an onion like a piece of fruit and sees nothing wrong with this. The 'Yes' response is perfect deadpan: no defensiveness, no explanation, just ownership. Highly original in its specificity and commitment. The joke is funnier on rewatch when you notice she's just been sitting there with this onion.

jokes.text: 'It's a ball of foil for my son.' — Michael explains what's in the foil while packing George Michael's lunch.
jokes.setup: Michael is packing a lunch. He has something wrapped in foil.
jokes.punchline: It's not food — it's a ball of foil, presumably for George Michael to play with or as a running gag item. The mundane explanation ('for my son') makes it stranger.
jokes.explanation: A mild visual oddity that generates a small laugh. The joke is in Michael's total sincerity about giving his teenage son a ball of foil. Not particularly crafted but serves as background texture of the show's absurdism.

jokes.text: Michael tells Tobias 'I never really noticed' about Jessie being cute — but the narrator immediately reveals he'd been working up the nerve to talk to her at the gym for weeks.
jokes.setup: Tobias asks if the new publicist is cute. Michael insists he never noticed.
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately reveals this is false — Michael has been noticing her at the gym and only recently got up the nerve to approach her.
jokes.explanation: The narrator-as-fact-checker is a reliable AD device and it works well here, establishing Michael's emotional dishonesty while poking fun at his romantic awkwardness. The setup for the gym flirting scene that follows.

jokes.text: Michael's flirting technique: 'Race you to the top of the hill.' Jessie brings a picnic lunch; Michael races anyway and arrives out of breath just as she's set up the picnic.
jokes.setup: Michael's flirting skills are described as 'rusty.' His opener is 'Race you to the top of the hill.'
jokes.punchline: He actually races — alone — leaving Jessie behind with the picnic lunch, arriving breathless and confused at the top while she calmly unpacks food.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully structured cringe comedy. The joke has two beats: the absurd flirting opener ('Race you to the hill'), and then the reveal that Michael took it literally and actually ran. The physical comedy is implied but vividly clear. Strong character integration — Michael's social awkwardness is the joke.

jokes.text: 'My relations are already a little too public. I'm Michael Bluth.' — Michael's pun in response to Jessie offering PR services.
jokes.setup: Jessie introduces herself and offers her public relations services.
jokes.punchline: Michael riffs on 'public relations' to reference his family's very public family drama — a terrible flirty pun, delivered earnestly.
jokes.explanation: A legitimately clever pun that works on its own but also characterizes Michael's rusty flirting (this is actually his best opener, which is damning with faint praise). The wordplay is clean, the self-deprecation is earned.

jokes.text: Michael catches himself saying 'She's a little cutie pie' after claiming he never noticed Jessie was cute — escalating through 'cute,' 'she's cute,' 'she's a cutie,' 'cutie pie.'
jokes.setup: Michael claimed he never noticed Jessie was attractive, then admits she's 'cute, I suppose.'
jokes.punchline: He can't stop himself — the admission escalates from 'cute' to 'cutie' to 'a little cutie pie,' each step making his earlier denial more ridiculous, before he adds 'But I never really noticed.'
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure is excellent — each step is slightly more ridiculous than the last, culminating in 'cutie pie' before the completely undermined callback to 'never really noticed.' The joke is about Michael's self-deception and total lack of self-awareness in the moment. Well-timed and well-constructed.

jokes.text: 'That's not an option. / 'Cause you're scared? / No, because of George Michael. He still hasn't gotten over losing his mother. I just want to get him through high school; then we'll deal with me.'
jokes.setup: Tobias asks why Michael won't just ask Jessie out.
jokes.punchline: Michael's noble-sounding explanation about getting George Michael through high school is immediately deflated by Tobias's next observation.
jokes.explanation: Setup for Tobias's follow-up punchline. Functional character work establishing Michael's self-sacrifice as a cover for fear/self-denial. Not a strong joke on its own.

jokes.text: 'So you didn't get any while you were in high school, and now you're not gonna get any while George Michael's in high school.'
jokes.setup: Michael explains he's delaying his romantic life until George Michael is through high school.
jokes.punchline: Tobias summarizes this with clinical bluntness — pointing out that Michael's celibacy spans from his own high school years through his son's, making it a decades-long dry spell.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's ruthless arithmetic is genuinely funny. By connecting Michael's high school years to his son's, he reveals the absurdity of Michael's self-imposed celibacy spanning an entire generation. The deadpan delivery (Tobias simply stating the math) makes it land harder. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: George Michael gets a job at the banana stand, but the family (including Jessie) think he means a 'real' job — 'like a plumber or something' — and are shocked.
jokes.setup: Jessie asks what George Michael does. The family assumes she means an adult career.
jokes.punchline: George Michael already has a job — at the banana stand — and the family reacts as if this is a surprising revelation. Lindsay's 'Oh! Duh. I thought you meant like a plumber or something' is the punchline.
jokes.explanation: Functional joke on family obliviousness — they don't know enough about George Michael to know his job. Also a running gag reference (the banana stand is a recurring element). Lindsay's 'like a plumber' reaction reveals her snobbery. Not one of the episode's stronger jokes.

jokes.text: George Sr. on the prison telelink: 'I've started a Torah study group. I'm very excited about it. Looks like I have some possible converts.'
jokes.setup: Jessie sets up a telelink to the prison so the family can hear from George Sr. as part of the PR strategy.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s first contribution is announcing his prison Torah study group with genuine enthusiasm — implying he's converting hardened criminals to Judaism as a side hustle.
jokes.explanation: The joke works because it plays against type: George Sr. was not previously established as religious, so prison religion as opportunistic rehabilitation is funny. The possibility of prison converts adds absurdist texture. Solid character comedy.

jokes.text: 'Who doesn't love the Jews?' — Lucille, enthusiastically endorsing George Sr.'s Torah group as good PR.
jokes.setup: Jessie says the prison Torah group is sympathetic for PR purposes.
jokes.punchline: Lucille's enthusiastic rhetorical question ('Who doesn't love the Jews?') is both genuinely funny and deeply uncomfortable — the kind of statement that reveals casual prejudice dressed up as support.
jokes.explanation: Classic Lucille: enthusiastic bigotry disguised as a compliment, delivered with total sincerity. The joke is in the unselfconsciousness — she thinks this is a kind thing to say. Character integration is very high; only Lucille would say this and mean it positively.

jokes.text: Tobias mishears 'single' as 'straight' and then backpedals: 'She said single, correct? I thought l... I didn't quite hear that. The reception is bad.'
jokes.setup: Jessie says Michael is the most 'likable' because he's one of the few 'attractive and straight men in this town.' Tobias visibly reacts.
jokes.punchline: Tobias first volunteers 'That certainly leaves me out' — implying he heard 'straight' — then desperately claims she said 'single' and blames the phone reception.
jokes.explanation: One of the show's signature running gags — Tobias's obliviously closeted homosexuality. The layered joke: he first admits he's not straight, then panics and claims bad reception. 'She said single' is a desperate cover. Rewatch bonus because viewers who know the show immediately recognize this as part of the larger Tobias arc. Strong character integration — this is purely a Tobias joke.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'There are very few intelligent, attractive and straight men in this town.' Then realizing: 'Well, that certainly leaves me out.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is making an argument about Michael's desirability in the dating market.
jokes.punchline: Tobias is mid-argument about straight men being rare when he catches himself and acknowledges 'that certainly leaves me out' — a moment of surprising self-awareness that also functions as a running joke about his sexuality/identity.
jokes.explanation: This is a signature Tobias moment — he accidentally reveals/confirms his queerness while mid-argument, completely unaware of the implication on one level but seemingly aware on another. 'That certainly leaves me out' is perfectly calibrated: it's a confession disguised as a logical deduction. The character integration is nearly perfect. One of the most quotable Tobias lines in the episode. Justifies 8+ — could ONLY be Tobias, unexpected, and functions on multiple levels simultaneously.

jokes.text: George Sr. on the telelink quotes the Talmud — 'to the jackal as to an oxen' — then immediately asks 'Did it get a laugh?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is supposed to be helping with the family's PR by displaying his newfound religious sincerity.
jokes.punchline: His 'Talmudic wisdom' is transparently gibberish ('to the jackal as to an oxen'), and he immediately breaks character to ask if it landed — revealing he's performing religiosity for PR rather than living it.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: the nonsense quote is funny on its own, and 'Did it get a laugh?' reveals George Sr.'s cynicism about his supposed religious conversion. Character-specific (George Sr. as con artist even in prison) and well-structured. The nonsense Talmud quote is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Jessie reveals Michael has only had sex with 'like, four women' — as background information for why he's likable.
jokes.setup: The family is discussing Michael's likability as the 'face' of the family.
jokes.punchline: Jessie casually mentions his extremely limited sexual history as a PR attribute — but it lands as a devastating personal revelation shared publicly.
jokes.explanation: The laugh is in Jessie's casual deployment of intimate information as PR strategy. Sets up a later callback (George Michael's 'four women' comment at the end). Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Jessie tells Lindsay: 'You're a medical doctor and you're living an absurd fantasy as an actor. It's time to get real.' Tobias's response: 'Wow. It's tough talk, but I like it. You're saying land a major film.' Jessie: 'I'm saying get your medical license back.'
jokes.setup: Jessie gives Tobias a frank assessment of his situation.
jokes.punchline: Tobias interprets 'get real' as 'land a major film' — still completely unable to process the advice. Jessie has to spell it out: get your medical license back.
jokes.explanation: Tobias's delusional filter is perfectly deployed — 'get real' = 'get a movie role' in his mind. The structure is clean: setup (frank advice), misdirection (Tobias's interpretation), correction (Jessie has to state the obvious). Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'I truly believe that the universe wants me to be an actor and not a doctor. I'm just waiting for a sign.' / 'Here's your cash.' / 'Universal Shuttle picks you up at 8:00.' / 'Any sign, really.'
jokes.setup: Tobias declares he's waiting for a 'sign' from the universe about whether to pursue acting.
jokes.punchline: Michael hands him the cash for his Boston trip and mentions 'Universal Shuttle' — Tobias hears 'Universe' and 'Universal' as cosmic confirmation, adding 'Any sign, really' to show how desperately he's reaching.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The escalation is perfectly timed: 'Here's your cash' → 'Universal Shuttle picks you up' → Tobias silently treats both as cosmic signs → 'Any sign, really.' The final beat ('Any sign, really') is devastating — it reveals both his desperation and his total lack of self-awareness. The wordplay on 'Universal' is organic, not forced. This is close to the 8.0 anchor and exceeds it in character specificity.

jokes.text: George Sr. on the telelink: 'Not wag our genitals at one another to make a point.' — followed by 'Thought provoking.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is apparently mediating an argument about the seder plate among his Torah study group in prison.
jokes.punchline: Whatever the argument was, George Sr.'s solution apparently involves a rule against genital display — a hilariously specific ruling that tells us everything about prison Torah study.
jokes.explanation: The absurdist specificity ('wag our genitals at one another to make a point') earns the laugh through unexpectedness. The follow-up 'Thought provoking' as a straight reaction adds a dry layer. Strong on originality — we've never heard this particular ruling from a prison rabbi before.

jokes.text: 'Hanukkah can be spelled so many ways. Oh, God!' — Lucille has a crisis over Hanukkah spelling during the PR strategy discussion.
jokes.setup: The family is trying to capitalize on George Sr.'s Torah group for PR.
jokes.punchline: Lucille, presumably asked to write something, erupts over Hanukkah spelling variants — treating this as a catastrophic problem.
jokes.explanation: A minor absurdist moment — Lucille's disproportionate distress about Hanukkah spelling is funny but not one of the episode's stronger beats. The 'Oh, God!' sells it.

jokes.text: Buster enthusiastically volunteers to help ('Right here! Ready to go. At your service. Get me out there!') and Jessie tells him to stay in because 'People find you odd and alienating.' He replies: 'I shall be neither seen nor heard. Watch me.'
jokes.setup: Jessie is assigning roles to each family member. Buster eagerly wants to participate.
jokes.punchline: Jessie shuts him down with clinical honesty ('People find you uneasy'). Buster's response — 'I shall be neither seen nor heard. Watch me.' — is a callback to the Milford School credo and a perfect self-defeating statement (you can't watch someone who is neither seen nor heard).
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. Buster's line is both a callback to the Milford credo AND a self-contradictory statement (he says 'watch me' while claiming to be neither seen nor heard). The show's layered writing is on full display: the phrase has been established, paid off with the flashback, and now Buster has internalized it as a coping mechanism. The line 'You can always tell a Milford man' appearing as a tag confirms the joke. Near-exceptional — justifiably scores near the top.

jokes.text: 'You can always tell a Milford man.' — Narrator tags Buster's exit after the 'neither seen nor heard' moment.
jokes.setup: Buster has just invoked the Milford credo and presumably walked out silently and invisibly.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's dry tag ('You can always tell a Milford man') functions as both an ironic compliment and a self-evident truth — if you can always tell them, they're not exactly invisible.
jokes.explanation: Five words that function as a perfect sardonic button on the Buster/Milford running gag. The phrase becomes a recurring tag in the episode (used again at 15:22) which elevates it. Extremely economical. The irony that Milford men are always recognizable despite being trained to be invisible is a quiet but real joke.

jokes.text: Tobias on the Universal Shuttle, complaining about how many stops it makes: 'Come on! We're stopping again? How many more stops are we gonna make? Honestly, I haven't even seen this part of Los Ang... Is that snow?' / 'Oh, my God.'
jokes.setup: Tobias chose the $12 shuttle over a limo. He's bragging about the value.
jokes.punchline: The shuttle has made so many stops that Tobias has ended up somewhere far outside LA — possibly out of state — seeing snow for the first time on his way to the airport.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of shuttle stops ending in Tobias apparently in the mountains/another state is a solid absurdist payoff for the cheap-shuttle setup. The 'Is that snow?' moment is the peak.

jokes.text: Tobias recognizes Carl Weathers on the shuttle: 'You are Carl Weathers, the actor. I went to San Francisco to attend your stage fighting workshop. But you never showed up.' Carl: 'I got bumped from that flight.' 'Hell, they'll give you $300 if you get bumped. It's this crazy loophole in the system that the wrong guy discovered.'
jokes.setup: Tobias finds himself sharing a shuttle with Carl Weathers.
jokes.punchline: Carl Weathers' excuse for not showing up to the workshop is that he was bumped from a flight — but rather than apologizing, he enthusiastically describes the $300 compensation as a 'loophole' he discovered, implying he does this on purpose.
jokes.explanation: The introduction of Carl Weathers as a cheapskate celebrity is a brilliant comic creation. His pride in the flight-bump 'loophole' immediately establishes his character as a hustle-obsessed miser despite being a recognizable actor. 'The wrong guy discovered' is a perfectly specific touch.

jokes.text: 'Guess where I won't be going.' — Carl Weathers, after revealing he got bumped intentionally from the Boston flight.
jokes.setup: Carl Weathers missed Tobias's workshop because he voluntarily got bumped from a flight for $300.
jokes.punchline: The implication — he's now on the same shuttle going nowhere near Boston — is delivered with complete self-satisfaction.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly timed deadpan button on the flight-bump revelation. The economy is exceptional — five words that confirm both his cheapness and his lack of a fixed destination. Carl Weathers as character is already earning laughs.

jokes.text: Carl Weathers after Tobias says he's waiting in the shuttle: 'Guess where I won't be going.' — implying Carl will stay on the shuttle to get bumped again
jokes.setup: The discussion of getting bumped from flights establishes Carl's modus operandi.
jokes.punchline: Carl signals he too will skip the airport for the bump rebate opportunity — he's essentially a professional airline-bumpee.
jokes.explanation: Deadpan continuation of the Carl Weathers character joke. The implication that Carl actively avoids flying to collect bumping fees is a good absurdist character detail. Economy is high.

jokes.text: Carl Weathers charges Tobias exactly $1,100 for acting classes — the precise amount Tobias has for his plane ticket. 'Check this out. $1,100 is exactly what I charge for acting classes.' / 'No, it isn't!' / 'Yeah.' / 'What are the chance... Universe, you've done it again.'
jokes.setup: Tobias mentions he has $1,100 — enough for a plane ticket to Boston to get his medical license back. Carl Weathers hears this and offers acting classes.
jokes.punchline: Carl's fee is conveniently and obviously exactly $1,100 (Tobias calls this out: 'No, it isn't!'). Tobias, desperate for a sign, interprets this as the universe speaking — having already associated 'Universal Shuttle' with cosmic guidance.
jokes.explanation: This is a brilliantly constructed comedic escalation. The callback to the 'sign from the universe' is paid off perfectly — Tobias's desperation is so complete that Carl Weathers obviously lying about his fee (Tobias even calls it out: 'No, it isn't!') still counts as a cosmic sign. The structure is flawless: setup (waiting for a sign) → false sign (Universal Shuttle) → escalation → payoff (obviously fraudulent acting class fee). 'Universe, you've done it again' is a highly quotable button. Justifiably among the episode's best.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'No, it isn't!' Carl: 'Yeah.' / Tobias: 'Universe, you've done it again.'
jokes.setup: Tobias briefly snaps to reality when Carl announces the suspiciously convenient $1,100 price.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's moment of clarity ('No, it isn't!') is immediately abandoned for magical thinking ('Universe, you've done it again') — he chooses to believe over knowing. Callbacks the 'waiting for a sign' setup from 07:34.
jokes.explanation: 'Universe, you've done it again' is the perfect line — it encapsulates Tobias's magical thinking, his self-deception, and his willful abandonment of rationality in service of his dream. The callback to 'waiting for a sign' completes the arc. The economy is perfect: three short lines tell an entire psychological story. This is the kind of line that becomes a catchphrase and represents Tobias at his absolute best as a comedic character. Easily one of the episode's top jokes.

jokes.text: Gob at the nursing home: 'I'm gonna need a volunteer for my next illusion, the Aztec Tomb!' Old volunteer appears. Gob: 'Or box. Box is... fine.'
jokes.setup: Gob is doing charity work at a nursing home with his magic act.
jokes.punchline: The volunteer's reaction (presumably a very elderly person's concern about a 'tomb') prompts Gob to immediately downgrade from 'Aztec Tomb' to 'box' — a deflation of his own grandiosity.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Gob's involuntary deflation of his own illusion. 'Aztec Tomb' is so clearly the wrong choice at a nursing home that his pivot to 'box is fine' is an excellent character beat — Gob's grandiosity immediately caving to practicality. The ellipsis ('box is... fine') is perfect.

jokes.text: Gob instructs the elderly volunteer how to do the panel trick: 'There's a panel. Just flip it around, curl up behind it, don't make any noise.' — and the volunteer apparently follows through.
jokes.setup: Gob is performing his Aztec Tomb illusion with an elderly volunteer.
jokes.punchline: Gob is directing a very elderly person to curl up in a box and be silent — and they do it, which sets up the later disappearance joke.
jokes.explanation: Setup more than payoff — this moment exists to establish the disappearance. The dark comedy of having an elderly man curl up in a box labeled 'Tomb' is mildly funny on its own, with the cringe coming from the real-world implications. Rewatch bonus for setting up the Earl Milford reveal.

jokes.text: During the illusion, after asking 'Ancient lore has it the Aztec gods put a curse on any man who dare—' the assistant reveals: 'He's gone.' The old man has actually vanished.
jokes.setup: Gob performs the Aztec Tomb with an elderly volunteer. The trick is supposed to make the volunteer 'disappear' (hide behind the panel).
jokes.punchline: The volunteer has actually disappeared — not hiding, but genuinely gone — which Gob interprets as a trick failure when it's actually much more alarming.
jokes.explanation: The misdirection works because we assume 'gone' means the trick worked, but the panic in 'What?' and 'He's gone' suggests real disappearance. The dark implication (elderly man missing from a nursing home) adds to the cringe. Sets up the Earl Milford revelation.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Buster was also doing his share for the family.' — cut to shot of Buster, presumably just sitting somewhere doing nothing visible
jokes.setup: The narrator announces Buster is contributing to the family's PR effort.
jokes.punchline: The cut to Buster (who was told to be invisible) presumably shows him successfully being neither seen nor heard — existing in a corner or background, 'contributing' by doing nothing at all.
jokes.explanation: The visual gag here is that Buster's 'share' for the family is the absence of presence — the joke is in what we DON'T see. Strong callback to the Milford thread. The narrator framing nothing as 'doing his share' is dry and effective.

jokes.text: At the bar, Lindsay does the Cloudmir Vodka promotion: 'Wow! This Cloudmir's making me think fuzzy. I have almost no judgment at all.' Then she orders two more.
jokes.setup: Lindsay has been hired to promote Cloudmir Vodka by ordering it conspicuously at a hot bar.
jokes.punchline: Her delivery of the promotional line ('making me think fuzzy... almost no judgment at all') is so on-the-nose as an advertisement that it's clearly embarrassing — and she immediately orders more, suggesting the promotion is working all too well on herself.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the terrible quality of Lindsay's promotional delivery and her lack of self-awareness. 'Almost no judgment at all' as a selling point is funny. Michael's 'Only one with a real job' callback is the better laugh.

jokes.text: The photographer takes a candid photo of Michael and Jessie as Jessie says 'Get one of Michael and me on a date.' Michael is shocked: 'A date? This is a date now?'
jokes.setup: Michael thinks dinner with Jessie is a business meeting. Jessie casually calls it a date while directing a photographer.
jokes.punchline: Michael's shocked reaction — 'A date? This is a date now?' — reveals he had no idea and is immediately thrown. The photographic documentation makes it more embarrassing.
jokes.explanation: Solid situational comedy — Michael being blindsided by the 'date' designation while being photographed is a good beat. Not exceptional but functional.

jokes.text: Michael flirts by saying 'Can't it be a little bit of both?' about the date/business question, then asks 'How will I know which part of it is business?' Jessie teases him and Michael ends with 'Show me business again.'
jokes.setup: Jessie and Michael debate whether their dinner is a date or business.
jokes.punchline: Michael's discomfort leads to the absurd request to 'show me business again' — a euphemistic suggestion that is simultaneously sexually awkward and genuinely confused.
jokes.explanation: The 'show me business' line works as both innuendo and a genuine expression of Michael's confusion. The double meaning is there but not labored. Decent wordplay.

jokes.text: George Michael tries to fix the TV: 'Oh, yeah, I tried that earlier. I think it's still hooked up to the prison.' 'So when those guys kept saying, "Hey, you, boy," that was me? I was the boy?'
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby are watching TV (or trying to). The TV is somehow connected to the prison feed.
jokes.punchline: Maeby reveals the TV has been showing the prison, and George Michael realizes the inmates were apparently addressing him through the screen — 'I was the boy?' The horror and confusion are the joke.
jokes.explanation: Genuinely absurdist and darkly funny. The image of George Michael, a sweet teenage boy, watching prison TV while inmates called out to him is both bizarre and disturbing. 'I was the boy?' captures his dawning horror perfectly. The joke works on the absurdist premise and the character — George Michael's sheltered innocence colliding with prison reality.

jokes.text: Maeby tells George Michael that going to Milford means they won't be at the same high school. George Michael: 'Man, it just keeps getting worse.' Maeby: 'But he's my Dad. I don't want to disappoint him.' George Michael: 'Man. You and I are so different. It's like we're not even related.'
jokes.setup: George Michael and Maeby discuss the Milford School. Maeby expresses filial duty about her father.
jokes.punchline: George Michael says 'It's like we're not even related' — which, given that they are first cousins with an incestuous attraction (a major show arc), is darkly ironic on multiple levels. Maeby's 'That'd be amazing' is the follow-up.
jokes.explanation: A classic AD layered joke: on the surface it's George Michael expressing that he and Maeby have different values, but underneath it's a reference to his (and her) wish that they weren't cousins so they could act on their feelings. 'That'd be amazing' from Maeby confirms she's on the same wavelength. High rewatch bonus — you need the full series context to appreciate the full depth.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'That'd be amazing.' — responding to George Michael's 'It's like we're not even related.'
jokes.setup: George Michael says 'It's like we're not even related' as an expression of their philosophical differences.
jokes.punchline: Maeby's 'That'd be amazing' reveals she's interpreting it romantically — wishing they weren't related would solve the cousin problem.
jokes.explanation: Three words that carry enormous comedic and dramatic weight. The economy is exceptional — the entire George Michael/Maeby cousin-crush subplot is distilled into 'That'd be amazing.' The dark subversiveness of a teenager wishing she weren't related to her crush-cousin is handled with perfect lightness. High rewatch value because it's easy to miss the full implication on first viewing.

jokes.text: Michael comes home late from his 'date' with Jessie and tells George Michael that Jessie 'booked Gob a gig at Earl Milford's nursing home.' George Michael: 'Oh.' Michael: 'She's kinda something, isn't she?' George Michael: 'Yeah, I guess.'
jokes.setup: Michael is excited about Jessie's work. He cites as evidence that she booked Gob a nursing home gig.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's underwhelmed 'Oh' and 'Yeah, I guess' are perfect deflations of Michael's enthusiasm — a nursing home magic show is not exactly evidence of PR brilliance. The irony is that this gig will cause a massive crisis.
jokes.explanation: Functional scene — Michael's misplaced pride in Jessie's accomplishment (booking Gob at a nursing home) and George Michael's dry response. Rewatch bonus because we know the nursing home gig is about to go catastrophically wrong.

jokes.text: Michael and George Michael both over-emphatically deny that Michael is dating Jessie: 'You're not dating her.' 'No, I am not dating her.' 'Absolutely not dating her.' 'Just you and me. The Bluth boys.' 'Bluth boys.'
jokes.setup: After a date/non-date with Jessie, Michael checks in with George Michael about their relationship.
jokes.punchline: Both protest so much and so identically that their denial becomes comical — 'Absolutely not dating her' is more emphatic than the situation requires, revealing how much both of them are trying to convince themselves.
jokes.explanation: The mirrored denial structure works as character comedy — Michael's dishonesty with himself, George Michael's uncertainty about how to respond. The synchronized 'Bluth boys' is a small sweet/pathetic button.

jokes.text: Michael tells Jessie 'This might be moving a little bit too fast for him... for us. Me. Uh, for me.' — catching himself in real time.
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to let Jessie down gently, citing George Michael as the reason.
jokes.punchline: He starts with 'him' (George Michael), realizes he's hiding behind his son, and fumbles through 'for us... Me. Uh, for me.' — the self-correction in real time is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A nice character moment — Michael's inability to use George Michael as a shield collapses in real time as he hears himself. The stumbling self-correction is more honest (and funnier) than his earlier noble framing. Well-integrated into Michael's arc.

jokes.text: Jessie fires back: 'So the two months on the bike, that was, what, for my health?'
jokes.setup: Michael claims their gym relationship has been professional.
jokes.punchline: Jessie reveals she's been riding a stationary bike near him for two months — which she clearly did for him, not for fitness — calling out his revisionist history with sharp sarcasm.
jokes.explanation: A sharp, economical retort. The image of Jessie dedicating two months of gym time to getting close to Michael — only to be told it was 'professional' — is both funny and a little sad. The sarcasm lands cleanly.

jokes.text: Jessie quits: 'Let's take two steps back and why don't you find yourself a new publicist.' Michael calls after her: 'Jessie.' She keeps walking. Michael: 'No. I was just saying your name as you walked away. I didn't...' Long pause. 'I have no follow-up.'
jokes.setup: Jessie walks out on Michael after he tries to pump the brakes on their relationship.
jokes.punchline: Michael calls 'Jessie' after her, then — when she doesn't stop — clarifies that he was just saying her name, not actually calling her back. Then admits he has 'no follow-up.' The self-awareness of his own awkwardness is devastating.
jokes.explanation: 'I have no follow-up' is one of the episode's best lines. The structure is excellent: Michael does the thing (calls after her), realizes what he's done, tries to retroactively deny it (just saying her name), and then admits defeat with perfect self-deprecating honesty. 'I have no follow-up' is extremely quotable and reveals Michael's romantic helplessness better than any elaborate scene could. Justifiably scores among the episode's best.

jokes.text: Gob calls Michael in crisis: 'I need her to spin something for me.' Michael: 'Well, that's too bad because I think she quit. What do you need her to spin?' Gob: 'Murder.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells Gob that Jessie quit. Gob says he needs her to spin something.
jokes.punchline: The pause before 'Murder' — and the word itself, standing alone — is perfectly timed dark comedy. The single-word punchline is maximally effective.
jokes.explanation: Structurally near-perfect. The setup ('what do you need her to spin?') primes us for a manageable problem, and 'Murder' delivers maximum subversion. The single-word punchline is exemplary economy — Gob doesn't explain, doesn't contextualize, just drops 'Murder' into the phone call. One of the episode's cleanest joke structures. The dark/absurd combination is quintessential AD.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Murder, huh? Who died?' Gob: 'My career.' Michael: 'I'm going to go home now.' Gob: 'I lost a guy.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks who died after Gob says he needs to spin 'murder.'
jokes.punchline: Gob dramatically says 'My career' — a hyperbolic non-answer. Michael's disgusted response ('I'm going to go home now') is immediately undercut by Gob's actual admission: 'I lost a guy.' The real problem is worse than the metaphorical one.
jokes.explanation: Three-beat structure: 'Who died?' → 'My career' (dramatic misdirection) → Michael gives up → 'I lost a guy' (the real, worse answer). 'I'm going to go home now' as a reaction beat is excellent — Michael's resignation as humor. Then the escalation back to the actual problem ('I lost a guy') reverses the misdirection. Expertly layered.

jokes.text: 'You can always tell a Milford man.' — Second use, after Gob reveals Earl Milford was the man in the box.
jokes.setup: Gob reveals the man he 'lost' was Earl Milford — a Milford man. Gob notes he was 'spectacularly quiet.'
jokes.punchline: The narrator repeats the tag line — 'You can always tell a Milford man' — confirming the running gag and landing the callback with dry satisfaction.
jokes.explanation: The second deployment of 'You can always tell a Milford man' is more satisfying than the first because it now has the full weight of Earl's appearance as payoff. The running gag is now established and the episode will use it one more time. The economy of five words earning a big laugh is excellent AD craft.

jokes.text: Tobias wonders aloud whether he's cut out to be 'a De Niro or a Regis or a Pinkett-Smith or what have you.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is having doubts about his acting dream.
jokes.punchline: His list of acting aspirations goes 'De Niro... Regis... Pinkett-Smith' — a list that starts with a serious actor, pivots to a talk-show host, and ends with a hyphenated name delivered with false equivalence. 'Or what have you' caps the absurdity.
jokes.explanation: The list joke works through the escalating absurdity of who Tobias considers his acting peers: De Niro (credible), Regis (not an actor), Pinkett-Smith (unclear which one, suggests confusion). The 'or what have you' dismisses his own aspirations even as he states them. Well-integrated into Tobias's delusion.

jokes.text: Tobias's 'acting class' with Carl Weathers: 'I don't know what your police captain told you about me, but I'm a different breed of cop. I'm from the streets, and I'm the last cop you're ever gonna wanna mess with in a darkened alley.' Tobias: 'Dark alley. And l...'
jokes.setup: Tobias is paying $1,100 for Carl Weathers' 'acting classes.' The lesson appears to be Carl performing a scene.
jokes.punchline: Carl is essentially doing a generic tough-cop monologue as his acting instruction — and Tobias's only contribution is a minor script note ('dark alley'). The 'class' is Carl entertaining himself.
jokes.explanation: The joke is that Carl Weathers' 'acting class' is just Carl performing for himself, and Tobias is paying $1,100 to be his audience. Tobias's only note ('dark alley') is pathetically timid. Character-specific and part of the excellent Carl Weathers cheapskate-celebrity running gag.

jokes.text: Carl Weathers: 'Dreams are worth fighting for. Now, are you gonna be a fighter, or are you gonna be a doctor?' — delivered as if being a doctor is a dishonorable option.
jokes.setup: Tobias is wavering between acting and medicine.
jokes.punchline: Carl Weathers frames 'being a doctor' as the coward's path — a complete inversion of normal values — entirely to keep Tobias paying for acting lessons.
jokes.explanation: Carl Weathers as a mercenary motivational speaker who inverts conventional wisdom to keep his client is funny. 'Are you gonna be a fighter, or are you gonna be a doctor?' is an excellent reframing of the question — the subtext (Carl needs this $1,100) makes the manipulation funnier.

jokes.text: Tobias reveals his wife works at a restaurant. Carl immediately pivots: 'Do they get a shift meal or do they just pay half-price on select menu items?' — leading them to go to the restaurant immediately.
jokes.setup: Tobias emotionally mentions he should go tell his wife he's following his dreams. Carl tries to be supportive.
jokes.punchline: Carl's instant pivot from emotional support to food discounts reveals his true motivation — he's not a life coach, he's hungry and looking for a deal. They immediately go to the restaurant.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best Carl Weathers moments. The joke crystallizes his character: every emotional conversation is secretly about finding a free or discounted meal. 'Do they get a shift meal or do they just pay half-price on select menu items?' is a brilliant specificity — the distinction between shift meals and half-price menus reveals he's thought about this extensively. This joke establishes Carl Weathers as a true comic creation for the series. Justifiably near the top.

jokes.text: The press gets hold of Gob's story. Gob: 'I put him in a box. I didn't kill him, all right? And don't edit this for your broadcast so it looks like I'm screaming, "I killed Earl Milford."' News anchor: 'Startling confession, tonight at 11:00.'
jokes.setup: Gob specifically instructs the news crew not to edit his denial into a confession.
jokes.punchline: They immediately do exactly that — 'Startling confession, tonight at 11:00.' The meta-awareness of Gob predicting the edit but being powerless to stop it is perfect.
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly structured media-satire joke. Gob's attempt to preempt the edit by narrating what they shouldn't do is the setup; the news anchor immediately using 'startling confession' is the perfect payoff. The joke is that Gob's specific warning becomes the script for his own undoing. Structurally excellent — the setup contains the punchline's instructions.

jokes.text: Earl Milford emerges from the equipment: 'Don't send me back. They abuse us. It's not pleasant.'
jokes.setup: Gob discovers Earl Milford has been hiding in his magic equipment since the nursing home show.
jokes.punchline: Earl Milford — a Milford man — begs not to be returned, claiming abuse. The 'neither seen nor heard' school apparently breeds people desperate to escape from being seen and heard but also completely unable to advocate for themselves.
jokes.explanation: The discovery of Earl Milford in the equipment is a good payoff to the 'he's gone' beat. His complaint ('They abuse us. It's not pleasant.') is darkly funny — the passive construction ('they abuse us') is perfectly understated for a Milford man. The callback to Milford values is implicit.

jokes.text: Gob tells Earl he needs to return him to clear his name. Earl: 'You'll have a new name: Hero.' Gob considers it: 'Hero.' Pause. 'That could work for me.'
jokes.setup: Gob insists on returning Earl to the nursing home to clear his name.
jokes.punchline: Earl offers 'Hero' as an alternative name/identity. Gob — whose vanity is his defining trait — genuinely considers it. 'That could work for me' is the perfect Gob response: he'd trade his real name for a flattering one.
jokes.explanation: Pure Gob. The joke is his immediate vanity overriding the practical situation — rather than focusing on clearing his name, he's considering 'Hero' as a rebranding. 'That could work for me' is an understatement that reveals his ego completely. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Although he was spectacularly quiet in there.' — about Earl Milford hiding in the Aztec Tomb for an extended period.
jokes.setup: Gob explains that Earl disappeared during the trick.
jokes.punchline: 'Spectacularly quiet' — a callback to the Milford credo. Earl, as a Milford man, was perfectly trained to be neither seen nor heard, which is why Gob never noticed him in the box.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback. 'Spectacularly quiet' as a tribute to a Milford man's training — applied to the absurd situation of hiding in a magic box — is perfectly economical. The word 'spectacularly' paired with 'quiet' is a nice contradiction. Rewatch bonus for seeing how the Milford gag threads through the entire episode.

jokes.text: Jessie visits George Michael at the banana stand and blames him for his father's unhappiness: 'Daddy lost his shot at happy, and it's all your fault, Opie.'
jokes.setup: Jessie, having been rejected by Michael, goes to confront his teenage son.
jokes.punchline: She lays full blame on a teenage boy for his father's romantic life and calls him 'Opie' — a cutting reference to his wholesome, overly responsible persona. The inappropriateness of the confrontation is the joke.
jokes.explanation: 'Opie' is a well-chosen nickname — it captures George Michael's earnest, small-town-boy quality while being condescending. The joke is the sheer inappropriateness of a grown woman confronting a teenager about his father's love life. Cringe comedy that works.

jokes.text: The narrator: 'Jessie had gone too far, and she had best watch her mouth.' — Then immediately: 'Michael's about to become the most unlikable man in town. Check the papers.'
jokes.setup: After Jessie threatens Michael's reputation via the paper, the narrator seems to defend Michael.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'she had best watch her mouth' sounds protective — then immediately the narrator reports that Jessie has already planted the damaging story, making the warning moot. The narrator's omniscience working against the characters.
jokes.explanation: The narrator functioning as a slightly biased commentator — taking Michael's side while reporting damage already done — is a nice meta touch. Not a standout moment but solid AD narrator work.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'She kind of reminds me of my mother.' — about Jessie after she's emotionally abused him.
jokes.setup: Jessie has just guilted and insulted George Michael at his banana stand job.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's comparison of this abusive adult to his deceased mother — delivered completely sincerely — is both darkly funny and character-revealing. It tells us about Michael's late wife without us meeting her.
jokes.explanation: Quietly devastating. In four words, the show implies that the beloved, sainted late mother was possibly not as gentle as Michael's idealization suggests. The dark comedy is in the casual delivery — George Michael isn't upset, he's almost nostalgic. Excellent economy and character specificity.

jokes.text: At Rud, a man offers Lindsay $2,000 to touch him. Lindsay is shocked — 'Oh, my God. My husband.' The man is with Tobias.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is working at Rud as the Cloudmir promoter. A man makes an inappropriate offer.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's 'Oh, my God. My husband.' — suggests Tobias is the man propositioning her, but the next lines reveal Carl Weathers is her acting teacher and Tobias is there for a discounted meal.
jokes.explanation: The surprise of the husband being someone other than expected works but is undercut slightly by the script's focus shifting to Carl Weathers. The '$2,000 to touch me' joke is a mild shock gag. The real comedy comes from what follows.

jokes.text: 'I buy all my cars at police auction.' Carl Weathers delivers this as a life lesson he's teaching Tobias.
jokes.setup: Tobias says Carl is teaching him 'valuable life lessons.'
jokes.punchline: The specific 'life lesson' is that Carl buys his cars at police auction — a budget tip from a celebrity delivered with TED-talk gravitas. Tobias adds 'He's full of stuff like that.'
jokes.explanation: One of the great Carl Weathers lines. 'I buy all my cars at police auction' is delivered as profound wisdom — the specificity (police auction, not just 'cheap') and Tobias's awed 'He's full of stuff like that' make it exceptional. The joke deepens Carl Weathers as a comic character: he's not just cheap, he's developed a philosophy of cheapness that he genuinely believes is wisdom. Extremely quotable, strong rewatch value, perfect economy. This approaches the 8.0 anchor and arguably matches it.

jokes.text: Carl Weathers: 'Give me a bourbon. Any room on that tab for me to jump on?' — to Lindsay, after the revelation that she's his acting student's wife.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is at work. Carl Weathers has arrived (presumably because of the free/discounted food angle from before).
jokes.punchline: Carl immediately asks if he can join Lindsay's tab — not asking for a free drink, asking to 'jump on' her existing tab, which is slightly more socially acceptable but still classic Carl.
jokes.explanation: Another Carl Weathers cheapskate beat that rewards the running gag. 'Any room on that tab for me to jump on?' is nicely specific — he's not begging, he's offering to share the administrative burden of the tab. Consistent with the character's financial philosophy.

jokes.text: Michael arrives at Jessie's, ready to recommit, saying 'I really want to make this work' — but is cut off by Lucille storming in: 'Get away from my son!'
jokes.setup: Michael has reconsidered and come to Jessie's to get back together.
jokes.punchline: Just as Michael is about to have a romantic breakthrough, Lucille appears and shuts it down with 'Get away from my son!' — treating Michael (a grown man) as a child needing protection.
jokes.explanation: The interruption structure is a reliable sitcom device but well-deployed here. Lucille's 'Get away from my son' frames Michael as a victim needing rescue from his own romantic life — perfectly consistent with her possessive relationship with him.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Hey, Mom. Kind of on a date here.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has burst in on Michael's attempt to reconcile with Jessie.
jokes.punchline: 'Kind of on a date here' — Michael's understatement about his mother's intrusion. The 'kind of' suggests uncertainty about whether this even counts as a date, referencing the episode's running uncertainty about Michael and Jessie's relationship status.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the date/not-a-date ambiguity is nice — 'Kind of on a date' rather than 'on a date' shows Michael still can't commit to the label. Functional and character-consistent.

jokes.text: The article Jessie planted: 'Lindsay's a combative, entitled princess.' Lindsay reads it and Michael confronts Jessie. The Bluth family food fight at Rud begins.
jokes.setup: Jessie planted a story meant to help the Bluth image but revealing their worst qualities.
jokes.punchline: The article's description of Lindsay — 'combative, entitled princess' — is accurate, which makes it funny. Lindsay's outrage ('I should hire somebody to kick your ass') proves the characterization correct. Then a food fight breaks out — echoing the Klimpy's brawl.
jokes.explanation: The food fight callback (they started the episode in a restaurant brawl, now they're in another one) is satisfying. Lindsay's response to being called a combative princess — threatening to hire someone to kick Jessie — is perfectly self-undermining irony.

jokes.text: Michael confronts Jessie about talking to George Michael: 'You said that to my son?' / Jessie: 'Yeah.' / Michael: 'You're on your own. Ladies?' — and the food fight begins.
jokes.setup: Michael is increasingly angry at Jessie for planting the story and confronting George Michael.
jokes.punchline: 'You're on your own. Ladies?' — Michael formally ceding Jessie to the women of his family for retribution. The 'Ladies?' is a perfectly Bluth way to delegate violence.
jokes.explanation: 'Ladies?' as a one-word cue for violence is excellent — economical, character-specific, and funny. Michael stepping aside and formally delegating the confrontation to Lucille and Lindsay is both cowardly and chivalric in the most absurd way. Strong economy.

jokes.text: Food fight breaks out at Rud. 'Food fight!' 'What are you doing?' 'Oh, my face!' 'I'm gonna get you guys! Stop!'
jokes.setup: Michael says 'Ladies?' and Lucille and Lindsay attack Jessie.
jokes.punchline: Full food fight erupts — 'Oh, my face!' being the highlight. This mirrors the Klimpy's food fight from earlier in the episode.
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy of the food fight is implied. As a visual moment it earns laughs but it's the weakest of the three fight sequences — we've seen it once already and the Klimpy's version had better character-specific dialogue. The callback structure is sound but the execution is less sharp.

jokes.text: News report: 'The press is having a love affair with hating the Bluth family, and they were the center of another brawl this evening. I'm looking at $50,000 worth of medical bills here.'
jokes.setup: The Bluths have been in the news all episode for bad behavior.
jokes.punchline: $50,000 in medical bills from a restaurant food fight is an absurd escalation that underlines how catastrophic the Bluths are. The reporter's flat delivery adds to the comedy.
jokes.explanation: The absurd specificity of the dollar amount ($50,000 from food) is the joke. Competent but not exceptional — it's a button on the food fight rather than a standalone strong joke.

jokes.text: The Milford School headmaster tells Michael: 'It looks like you won't be getting into this school. Are you disappointed?' Michael turns to George Michael: 'Well, are you disappointed?'
jokes.setup: The headmaster asks Michael if George Michael is disappointed about not getting in.
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately deflects the question to George Michael — revealing that Michael doesn't actually know his son's feelings, which has been the episode's point all along.
jokes.explanation: A nice structural moment — Michael's inability to speak for his son (without asking him) is revealed, which is slightly ironic given that he's been claiming all episode that his decisions are for George Michael. The joke is quiet but character-resonant.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'I didn't want to go to that school, and Jessie's a psycho.' Michael: 'That could've saved us a little bit of time.'
jokes.setup: After the entire episode's drama about the Milford School and Jessie, Michael finally asks George Michael how he feels.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's blunt summary ('I didn't want to go to that school, and Jessie's a psycho') collapses the episode's entire plot. Michael's resigned 'That could've saved us a little bit of time' is perfect.
jokes.explanation: An excellent episodic button. The simplicity of George Michael's summary ('I didn't want to go' / 'Jessie's a psycho') retroactively makes all the adults look ridiculous. Michael's 'That could've saved us a little bit of time' is the perfect resigned acceptance. Both the economy and the structure are excellent — the episode's drama collapses in two sentences. Strong payoff to the Milford arc.

jokes.text: George Michael tells Michael it's fine if he wants to date. Michael: 'Yeah? Thanks, buddy.' George Michael: ''Cause you know, I mean, four women, that is kind of sad.' Michael: 'All right. Thank you.'
jokes.setup: George Michael gives Michael permission to date. Michael is touched.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's permission is immediately undercut by his editorial comment: 'four women' is kind of sad. The callback to Jessie's earlier revelation about Michael's limited sexual history is landed by his own son.
jokes.explanation: The callback is satisfying — the 'four women' detail planted in the Jessie scene pays off here when George Michael uses it to simultaneously encourage and roast his father. Michael's 'All right. Thank you.' (trying to stop the conversation) is a perfect button.

jokes.text: Michael: 'That could've saved us a little bit of time.'
jokes.setup: George Michael has delivered the simple truth that he didn't want to attend Milford and that Jessie is 'a psycho,' revealing the entire episode's crisis was unnecessary.
jokes.punchline: 'That could've saved us a little bit of time' — Michael's dry understatement about the entire episode's plot being avoidable with one honest conversation.
jokes.explanation: Meta-commentary on the episode's plot delivered in perfect deadpan — the entire Milford/Jessie arc condensed into 'could've saved us a little time.' Economical and self-aware. Works as both character beat and structural joke.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'But, Dad, if you ever want to date anyone else, that's fine with me.' Michael: 'Yeah? Thanks, buddy.' George Michael: ''Cause you know, I mean, four women, that is kind of sad.' Michael: 'All right. Thank you.'
jokes.setup: George Michael encourages Michael to date and then cites a number as context.
jokes.punchline: 'Four women, that is kind of sad' — George Michael independently arrives at the same number/assessment that Lucille weaponized earlier (joke #36), revealing Michael's intimate history is apparently family knowledge passed down from Lucille to grandchildren.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Lucille's 'four women' comment — now delivered by George Michael, Michael's own son — is a devastating escalation. The implication is that Lucille has shared Michael's sexual history with his children, and George Michael agrees with the assessment. 'That is kind of sad' echoes Lucille's dismissal while being delivered with George Michael's genuine sympathy, which makes it funnier. The callback earns its place as a top-tier episode moment because it doubles the original joke's impact while adding new horror (his son knows). Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'I know you got married in college, but that was your sophomore year. You had your whole freshman year to...' Michael: 'That's enough honesty for now. Thank you. Good night.' George Michael: 'There must have been girls at parties.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is offering Michael romantic encouragement that has turned into an audit of his sexual history.
jokes.punchline: George Michael's attempt to find silver linings ('you had your whole freshman year') and Michael's increasingly desperate attempts to end the conversation, culminating in George Michael persisting after being told good night ('There must have been girls at parties').
jokes.explanation: The escalation is excellent — George Michael keeps going after being told 'Good night,' adding 'There must have been girls at parties' as if Michael simply hasn't thought of this. The inversion of parent/child roles (son conducting a sexual history review of his father) is both funny and character-specific. The scene builds perfectly from the permission-granting to the uncomfortable audit. 'That's enough honesty for now' is a quotable button.

jokes.text: On the next Arrested Development: Gob holds a press conference: 'And, now, prepare to call me "hero."' The old man is apparently still asleep in the equipment box: 'He's resting, so... Oh.'
jokes.setup: Tag scene — 'on the next Arrested Development.'
jokes.punchline: Gob's triumphant press conference is undercut by Earl Milford still being in the equipment and apparently not moving — 'He's resting, so... Oh.' The 'Oh' suggests he's either asleep or dead.
jokes.explanation: The tag extends the dark comedy of the Earl Milford situation — 'He's resting, so... Oh' is a classic AD trailing-off sentence that implies something much darker. The 'prepare to call me hero' callback is satisfying. Good button for the episode.

jokes.text: Buster moves to the kitchen. Tag scene with Buster silently relocating.
jokes.setup: Buster was told to stay out of the spotlight at the start of the episode.
jokes.punchline: The episode's final image of Buster is him 'moving to the kitchen' — he's taken 'staying in' so literally that his big news is a room change within the model home.
jokes.explanation: A quiet, perfect Buster button. His compliance with 'stay in' taken to its logical extreme — not just staying in, but relocating within the home as if it's an adventure — is consistent with his stunted character. The visual of Buster moving his things to the kitchen is funnier for being understated.

tmdb_overview: When George Michael is a rejected by a private school due to the family's reputation, Michael hires a publicist from his gym who he's been flirting with to change the public perspective of the Bluths. Meanwhile, Tobias is sent to Boston to reclaim his medical license.
insight: Season 1's lowest-impact episode relies on cringe over density, underperforming its 59-joke setup.
